Not only the run is a trials card but it’s mixed with a nightfall/dungeon aspect (fighting against the AI, figuring out map mechanics) then sprinkled with gambit (I describe Rooks as gambit invaders). Successfully exfiltrating with prestige or multiple superior loot is like reaching the OG lighthouse. Feels awesome. Marathon is awesome.

Corporate boardrooms. That’s what happened. C-suites and large shareholders decided that nothing could be modestly successful anymore. Absolutely everything has to have mind boggling returns, which causes the industry as a whole to chase trends set by the lightning-in-a-bottle projects. It’s the current problem pretty much every industry is facing right now.
As someone that worked in a game store, I can tell you the exact point gaming became mainstream: CoD MW2

Gaming turned mainstream in 2007. Gears of War, Halo 3, Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare, and a bunch of other entries rocketed into the stratosphere. The chickens didn't come home to roost until somewhere around 2014, though. That's when the modern environment really started to set in.

This reflects something I learned about media during my time at broadcasting college, when I interned for WTAM 1100 on the Mike Trivisanno Show I was operating the board for taking callers. I had a caller whose opinion was let's say "controversial", I asked one of the show hosts whether I should queue them or not. They told me "Controversy sells." to the best of my remembered paraphrasing, that stuck with me to the point where I decided not to pursue a career in broadcasting because of it.
Coming from someone who grew up in the early 90s and play a lot of video games  I believe anything after 2010 is when things really started to change in the gaming industry

I'm 41 started with a late Atari until my dad got me a Nintendo and so on. Gaming first change was Sega Saturn when they tried to make gaming more like an interactive movie. 

The 2nd and one that had the most damaging effect was in the 2000s when the cost of game development skyrocketed from million dollar projects into multi millions into hundreds. Requiring the need to sell a ridiculous amount of copies and DLC packs. That clearly evolved into battle passes, cosmetic, stat boost packs etc....

Those were my biggest changes in my gaming life.

In the pot goes:
1. Financial and political instability around the world.
2. Social media prioritising polarising and controversial statements.
3. CEO's and shareholders squeezing customers for more money every year.
4. Never being able to actually own anything you purchase.
5. Talent being constantly laid off instead of having great teams that work well.

And boom, you have a concoction that makes people highly agitated, jaded and resentful.

Edit: Obviously there is far more that goes into this. This is just some things I could think off the top of my head.
I remember camping out for consoles. Shit do I miss those days.
When did it become mainstream? 2007 with the release of the Xbox 360/Ps3 and the online networks becoming popular. Every game had a DLC pack back then.

Companies saw how easy and profitable it was to nickel/dime and exploit their customers. We had/have effectively zero regulation. This absolutely ravaged all the goodwill that was there before. It is an extreme environment and this results in an extreme reaction. Go find a chill/non-live service game where goodwill hasn't been eroded and you'll also find a very largely chill playerbase. Unfortunately most major games are live service now.

I'll also note that blaming GameStop from the live service push is absurd. Their used products only has a discount of like 5-10%. Before GameStop people would rent games, which I expect took a far larger share of the profits. The gaming industry was healthier during those eras than it is now.

I remember my last midnight release I went to the store for. It was Gears of War 3, nearly 15 years ago. When I went to the Gears 2 midnight release a few years before at the same Walmart, there were over 20 people waiting. At a rural Walmart! It was awesome. We were all excited.

For Gears 3? When I got to the store at 1145 and went back to the electronics, the only 2 other people who were waiting were 2 of MY good friends who loved Gears. Right at midnight another person came in right behind me. And it was just us 4. 

I knew then that the midnight release had gone the way of the Dodo. Truly a bittersweet moment. The gaming world has changed so much in the last 15 years, and not for the better. And fellas, I genuinely don't know if we can go back to the good days. I think Live Service Gaming is here to stay, and unless that bubble pops and crashes, shit ain't changing.

I was talking with someone about this a few months back, I really wish that more review companies were panel like how the old Nintendo Power reviews were. There would always be a thing at the front showing which reviewers were reviewing that month and the genres they typically liked, then you'd have like 4 people all give their score. And it was great, cause it meant for some games it would be like "oh thats a shooter and Jerry is reviewing this month and I know he loves shooters, I will see what he says first, but check how the others make comments on aspects they may have gripes about." It worked and a lot of games I ended up picking up were cause I would listen to the reviewers that played similar genres I liked. Just is hard now in the modern industry to make that sort of review pattern anymore with the sheer volume of games that come out on any platform any given day.

I remember watching a movie in a class at college that highlighted the fact that outrage was the most effective tactic to promote engagement on social media. the ragebait era is very real because the companies WANT it to be that way. anger at bad takes or mean spirited digs makes people want to tell the other guy off for it, and that desire has been captured and capitalized on through algorithms to just boost the hell out of that kind of content so people will get mad and put those engagement numbers up. Twitter's paid verification and engagement payouts for paid accounts incentivize it even further which is why bluecheck bots constantly steal and repost engagement bait tweets because they get paid for how many interactions they get.

The same can be said of a lot of modern live service games too. The suits don't care if the game is good, they care about income, average daily users, and how long they can keep the product going for money, and that kind of metric for success and failure for games has leaked into the very perception of how "good" a game is. To me it explains why the people who have been dogging on marathon since before it even came out are so obsessed with the player count, its because thats the only measure of "success" that they understand from dozens and dozens of big studio games living or dying because of their player population. Both games and social media as a whole are falling into a state of user optimization, where instead of trying to make the product good, or provide something actually worthwhile, they are focusing purely on numbers that never truly spell the whole story.

I think there were a combination of factors leading to this live service debacle we are in: During the early 2000's there was decreasing hardware costs and increasing speed/efficiency/space, increasing internet speeds, and widespread proliferation of the internet.  Then not just the games industry, but also movies and television wanted a slice of that reseller/rental market.  As digital seemed more viable, Netflix became a testing ground for replacing physical with digital in the TV/movies industry, and steam began proving the same model in gaming.  Now, it's not as common like it was in the early 2000's to go down to the game store with your friends and buy a used game or rent a game and spend all night playing it.  Everyone just buys their own copy off steam, and it's the way the number crunchers want it.

The problem with the Portal is that, from conception, it was designed as a system built for one purpose: to make content easier to produce, eliminating the need to create anything new. The developers' original idea was to use the Portal simply to remake existing content and redistribute it across seasons — giving players something "new" each season without actually having to build new content from the ground up. They could just recycle old stuff and call it a day.
The problem is that they failed at this spectacularly. First, players had always expected new content with each expansion, and the Portal simply did not deliver on that. Second, the developers seemed to think players would be perfectly fine with just recycling the same old content they already had. Not only were they wrong on both counts, they doubled down on the power grind — something nobody liked to begin with. And then when the idea of just replaying old content took hold, anything outside the Portal became irrelevant, which obviously wasn't what players had in mind.

TLDR
So the core issue with the Portal is that, from the start, it was designed as a content production shortcut — not as what it was originally pitched as: a system for players to customize their challenge and add their own modifiers.

Destiny 2 SHOULD have ended on The Final Shape. It was a tremendously superb ending for a Saga, and the perfect curtain call for D2. With the final scene being a "Teaser" for D3 (maybe).

D3 could have been introduced years later, with an aged up Zavala, a Bigger Last City - maybe not even the "LAST" City anymore, with expansions, settlements, and maybe even more towns/cities across Earth. As for the Campaign, it could have easily been the Echoes, which would lead into future expansions for each one.

Bring back old worlds (recovered from after Witness' Fall) - IO, Titan, Mars - new activities like; for example, clearing remnants of Dark Forces and Xivu Arath Hive across the System, expanding Settlements on other Planets as we once done in the Golden Age, slowly reaching across the Solar System once more.

Which would then allow future Expansions to introduce Worlds and Forces out of our System (EoF, Renegades, etc), beyond where Humanity had reached furthest from the Golden Age. And continuing the Campaign with each Echo getting their own Expansion and Story/Background/Conclusion, etc 
- The Nines, Kepler, and Maya Sundaresh's Machinations.
- Fikrul, the Scorn, and the homeworld of the Eliksni; Riis.
- Oryx, Savathun and Xivu, and a Hive-turned Torobatl.
Three different Expansions for each Echo, which would bring in New Worlds, Activities, Friends/Enemies, etc.

I was 6 years old when I first bought and played year 1 Destiny. Now I'm 18. This game literally defined over half of my life.

I joined the Destiny community with my game night group of friends right after The Taken King and Rise of Iron had just come out. I played the D2 Beta and Red War. Took a break, came back during Forsaken/Season of the Outlaw. Took a very long break, came back during Season of Arrivals (loved that season!) which got an extended time before Beyond Light launched. Played all year that year - up to the extended Season of the Wish just in time for Witch Queen. WQ was the best DLC for me - I love the Osmium Proto-Hive Family. (We still deserved a true ending for Xivu Arath! Who controls the Taken?) Loved the introduction of crafting our own weapons and Legendary Campaigns. Took another break, and then my buddy helped me with Lightfall campaign halfway through that year and Season of the Witch. Then I moved right before TFS came out.

Its been 2 years, and all I want to do is jump on with him again and play my Legendary TFS campaign to see how it all ends.

My Destiny journey, like so many others, has been bittersweet. Over 10+ years with this game.

RIP

I needed this. My story is not exactly similar, but also a bit in a way. When I was 13, my dad was assaulted while he was driving a bus. He was able to defend himself, but that moment changed who he was forever. He became a recluse, filled to his eye balls with ptsd, anxiety, depression and regret. He would lock himself in his room whenever family came over. He would never go out. He became cold and bitter. He may not have died, but he changed forever. My escape was also video games, and now that I'm in my 40s, they are still my escape, my safe place. Not everyone understands how gaming can actually help, and it's not just a 'hobby'. They're important. I appreciate you and the videos you are putting out, because i think we can all stand to hear these wise words. Big love to you friend 🫶

i remember the quiet night i first played D2. just on my own account on the xbox that already had it back in Season of the Worthy (idek what a season was at the time). perfect game i was just chilling with, doing random quests and finding loot to shoot more stuff and do more quests with. MIDA scout was my favorite thing ever just because it looked and sounded cool, and the smg was sick as well. ended up stumbling into finding Sturm (and Drang) as well. it ended up with me finding out that the Leviathan existed, and learning what a raid was. i looked into it, and ended up in an LFG that lasted me 4hrs and a few team rotations in which i was 100% that kid getting sherpa'd without knowing what he was doing and being taught Gauntlet on the fly. i ended up getting my singular 4hr completion where i obtained the Calus's Selected shader and a few pieces of cool looking loot. i honestly had no idea what was up with that robot alien guy named Calus, or why there were so many of him, but i knew i achieved something really cool when i finished the raid (even if i got hard carried.) i couldnt play the game again until years later on my own console in Beyond Light, where i began my commitment to what i thought was an amazing game at its peaks, and simply entertaining at its lows. Solstice, Dawning, Festival, Gaurdian Games, Campaigns, Raids, exotic missions, so many good memories and so much time invested into something that became a significant time in my life. i still have the friends that i wouldn't have known otherwise without D2. Edge of Fate made me quit with the game feeling like it's become a shell of what it was to me, and i didnt share the sentiment that the game should have ended. Final Shape felt pretty amazing to me again after Lightfall's performance, and i held hope for things to keep going in a good direction. something like getting to experience Cayde felt special to me since i wasn't there in his prime. i played EoF up to the point where i realized i genuinely couldn't find the fun in the game anymore. i liked how the story has developed, but the actual game left me drained and demotivated. it was heartbreaking but i totally quit the game for the better, rather than follow the sunk-cost fallacy and nostalgia. even now i wish i could install again and just have the fun i miss in it, but i know better. it's like when we needed Cayde in the Pale Heart, and eventually his time had to end; my need for the game had to come to an end, as peacefully as it could be handled. thank you Bungie (also i hate you), and to so many creators like Cross that helped me find my way through the game. this game was truly a blessed time in my life, and i hope everyone looks back fondly on their memories. Eyes up, Guardians.

"This is kind of on point."

Not only is it on point, but its what all the testers spent months since last summer trying to tell Bungie. Over and over. Onboarding is a huge wall, and we were seeing even testers giving up on the game after an hour. This was a constant topic of conversation on the testing Discord, and hundreds of us were trying to convince Bungie that the game needed better onboarding. A more robust tutorial. A set of actual starter missions that more clearly explained both gameplay and narrative. A firing range.

Yes, you can figure it out. But any barrier to entry drives people away.

The $200M-$400M budget was literally in your last video with the industry expert, Cross. YOU posted that. It’s the cost range of renting their studio and paying their employees for 5-6 years. And that is minus marketing costs.

21:08 It’s a combination of both. Marathon is hardcore while arc raiders captured the mainstream audience’s attention. I think Arc NAILS the social aspect of the genre but the gameplay loop and PvE engagements get old. It genuinely feels like a slot machine because I'm just opening containers and hoping that I find what I need. If I don't find what I need, I just move on to the next container, and the slot machine continues until I "hit".

You could argue that Marathon is similar because you're just opening containers to get loot, which is true, that is one of the things the genre is built around. But I feel like the in map events like Perimeter's Intercepts, Dire Marsh's Lockdowns, Outpost's access codes for Pinwheel... they all provide some sort of "guaranteed" loot and challenge that make me have to use my frontal cortex. For Arc Raiders, you can kill the "big Arc" for a challenge and they have the rotating map events but you never really need to engage with them unless you need specific Arc cores or want better item farming. But that's probably exactly why Arc resonates with so many people, it's a fun, social game that is easy enough to understand and doesn't force you to engage with the "harder" content unless you want to.

The compartmentalization inside Bungie is even stranger than most people realize.

Years ago, one of my old Destiny clan-mates had a friend who worked at Bungie, and he would play with us from time to time. (He never gave us any inside secrets, so don’t ask).

Sometime around the release of Beyond Light, he told us he had just switched over to the crucible team. So while we were playing crucible together, he asked us what kinds of things we liked or disliked about D2 Crucible at the time.

We each started bringing up issues like the balance of a certain weapon archetype, or certain supers/abilities being overpowered, or sight lines on a certain map… all the usual stuff PvP players would want to talk about.

With every single one of these topics, his response was “well, that’s not the crucible team”.

Weapon balance issues were handled by the weapons team, abilities by the sandbox team, map layout by the art team, etc.

Finally, one of us asked “well, what IS under the control of the crucible team”?” He explained to us that the crucible team was in charge of decisions like number of points required to win a match, match timers, map rotation within each playlist, etc. All important stuff to be sure, but I found it to be a very revealing conversation. 

Zooming out from the crucible, I have thought for years that D2 feels like it lacks a singular cohesive vision for the game. It feels like a game where the abilities, level design, weapon sandbox, etc are all being built in their own separate places and then thrown together. I know that at a certain level, that’s just kinda what game development is, but you’d hope for a strong singular vision that sits above all that which can help direct and change all the elements to help them fit together. I don’t think D2’s leadership has succeeded on that front in years.

Cross, the SCAR shines with appropriate build-crafting:

Triage can EMP enemies during Battery Overcharge if he cracks their Shields with a Volt Weapon. You've got that much already. But you want to compliment that with the right Mods and Cores. For example, Alternating Current is a chip that restores Health and Shields when damaging targets affected by EMP, and Electron Recapture Sinks is a Triage Core that instantly restores Volt Weapon charge when defeating Hostiles or downing Runners affected by EMP.

So, you pop Battery Overcharge going into a trade: You'll EMP them when you crack their shields, so they can't press their buttons, and you'll immediately start Leeching Health and Shields because they're EMPd with Alternating Current. And when you down them, you'll get Volt Charge back through Electron Recapture Sinks, so you can keep it going.

I agree with you, that the base SCAR isn't that hot.
But with the right build support, in the right Shell's hands -- its nasty.
Honestly the fact that it doesn't slow down is the biggest improvement. Being able to unload ~75 shots at full speed on a single battery is probably more sustained damage than an LMG

I'll die on this hill, all of the most recent new raid contests have been terrible. Bungie's equation has been to dial it up to 1000 (DP, SE) or to dial it down to 0 (RoN), and so far with Cryo Archives, they've chosen the former.

I honestly think by the end of wipe, not even 0.1% of the playerbase will even get all 6 AI subroutines unless they make some changes to the process. They need to make subroutines guaranteed in each vault, and security access acquisition needs to be more consistent. I can't count the number of times my team was so unlucky that there were 0 security access terminals in our starting zone, with only 1 or 2 security tags laying around. If I invest 300 hours into a wipe (I'm moderately skilled at this game imo), I feel like that should be enough to have beaten a compiler. I already am 130 hours (net worth around 850k credits) in with 0 subroutines and I don't think there's even a remote chance I'll be able to attempt the compiler by the end of the wipe, or maybe ever if Cryo Archives stays the way it is.

I don’t mind the difficulty at all granted I’ve only gotten out 1 time. What I’m not super keen on though is from a lore perspective. You only learn so much from in game events. Exfil with certain items gives you more lore behind everything the problem is the map is SO difficult and unforgiving that some people will only learn what happened because of streaming or YouTube videos. I’d like to learn these things on my own. Like maybe if you find something lore related or something you get the lore entry unlocked but the money/credits(if you get any) you don’t get until you get out with it or something. 

I definitely won’t be reaching the compiler at all and that’s cool. My only concern with the longevity of the game is that if it’s TOO hardcore people are not gonna stick around. It is kinda cool that there are gonna be weapons that some people will likely never get their hands on. 

Definitely not fun to do perimeter runs anymore though lol I’m trying to get more materials to upgrade factions and I’m running into gold shields. 

Don’t get me wrong here I enjoy the game. I got about 75 hours in it. I know at some point though it’ll burn out for sure. I can only do this hardcore PvP stuff for so long. 

Again I enjoy its difficulty and how harsh this game is. I just also know my limits as well and at some point I’ll stop caring trying to find out what actually happened on my own. I’m just not the biggest fan of lore being a gamble on if you can find out or not.

A panopticon is basically a cell block in a prison where the central warden area is literally in the center and viewed from the top down. It's like the thing you see in Prison of Elders when Cayde comes crashing down before his death. It has all the levers and switches needed to operate the cell block but also because its so high up, a prison is never able to tell if the wardens and crew are able to see them trying to do things they aren't supposed to be doing, which keeps them in check. Another popular example is the Guardians of the Galaxy prison break scenario when Groot steal the battery to get the crew off the ship.

I think this is really cool tech tbh. I work with 3d dccs and you'd be surprised what realism you can achieve with lighting tech and really good texture maps. To me this looks like it enhances texture maps and lighting but with a post rendering pass. This is definitely not a "phone filter". Of course this is a tech demo to wow stakeholders so for sure they'll crank it to 11 with 2 GPUs. It'll look ridiculous for gamers and I agree. But stakeholders don't care. My assumption is once it's available to the public and performance is resolved you can dial it up down or off, so I don't know what the big deal is. We'll see how it goes once it's released I guess. If not in a post render pass I can for sure see this tech used to enhance texture maps and lighting during production. Actually this is already the case with other solutions. AAA games are more and more expensive to make and advances like this are important to improve efficiency and quality. Just done incidentally and where it makes sense, being careful in avoiding trampling over artistic direction.

The camera thing isnt an issue technically it’s because when you go to finish someone, the game takes control of your camera. Then you, trying to regain control or set your mouse so you can face where you want when you’re done, aren’t expecting the game to bring your camera back to exactly where it was. Did you know?
In Japan, when advertising a fruit drink, there are strict rules based on actual fruit content.
If it’s under 5% juice?
You get cartoons.
If it’s between 5% and 99%?
Now you can show real fruit, but just whole fruit.
And if it’s 100% juice? Then you get the cross-section money shot. 
Now…Imagine we applied that standard to gaming, you wouldn’t get “from the makers of Halo.”
You’d get: “From the father’s brother’s nephew’s cousin’s former roommate…of a guy who once sat near someone…who may or may not have worked in the same building…as a team…that helped ship the last great Halo game…two decades ago.”

As someone who played destiny 1 Into destiny 2 I genuinely enjoyed the story every second of the way. It was genuinely such a spectacular time to be in the height of this game, but once final shape ended I was honestly satisfied with the story, I mean guys we L.I.T.E.R.A.L.L.Y. defeated the gone rogue dark god that was responsible for our collapse in the first place (The Witness), like we had built up all our years as destiny 1 veterans for this moment.
